food_subgroup: Fried chicken (boneless, smashed) with side salad

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Ayam Geprek. Closest defensible match is 'fried chicken food product' (FOODON_03315595); smashed preparation and Indonesian context are not captured.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR (Chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor. The smashed/geprek preparation style and side-salad component are not separately encoded in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Ayam Geprek or Indonesian smashed fried chicken. SNOMED CT clinical food coding does not extend to this dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a deep-fried smashed boneless chicken leg with a side salad, as typically sold at Indonesian fast-casual restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, leg, meat only, cooked, fried (SR Legacy ID 05066) and composite estimation with salad component
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for Ayam Geprek (Indonesian smashed fried chicken with side salad). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken leg (meat only, cooked, fried) combined with a proportional salad component estimate. Input per_100g values (energy 162.5 kcal, protein 11.25 g, fat 9.5 g, carbohydrate 8.75 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained with medium confidence. Singapore HPB Foodo database was consulted but does not contain a direct Ayam Geprek entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for mixed fried chicken with starch coating
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ayam Geprek. GI of 55 is estimated by analogy to battered/breaded fried chicken with a mixed carbohydrate component (wheat flour coating + side salad), consistent with entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. A GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55) per the University of Sydney classification scale. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (35 g from 400 g × 8.75 g/100g) / 100 = 19.25, rounded to 19.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content of 237.5 mg per 100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 950 mg sodium, representing ~41% of the WHO daily limit of 2,300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to 200 g or less per meal to keep sodium intake below 500 mg per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Wheat flour batter coating contributes refined carbohydrates (8.75 g/100 g); dietary fibre is low at 0.75 g/100 g, providing insufficient glycaemic buffering; estimated GI of 55 places the dish at the upper boundary of the low glycaemic category.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 200 g, request removal of the batter coating to reduce refined carbohydrate load, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to improve fibre intake and moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes saturated fat (2.5 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.13 g/100 g); cholesterol is 45 mg/100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 10 g saturated fat and 0.52 g trans fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid deep-fried preparations and request grilled or baked chicken to reduce saturated and trans fat intake; if consumed, limit to a 150–200 g portion and pair with a large vegetable side.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote systemic inflammation and may worsen joint symptoms; the dish may contain tomato (a nightshade) in the salad component.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried preparations due to pro-inflammatory AGE formation and monitor for joint symptom flares if nightshade vegetables are present in the side salad.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Geprek Original (Non-Spicy) is a popular Indonesian dish consisting of a boneless chicken leg that is marinated, coated in a seasoned wheat flour batter, deep-fried until golden and crispy, then smashed flat and served with a fresh side salad. The name 'geprek' derives from the Javanese word meaning 'to smash' or 'to crush'. This non-spicy version omits the chilli sambal that is characteristic of the traditional preparation, making it accessible to diners who prefer a milder flavour profile. The chicken is juicy and tender on the inside with a crispy, savoury exterior. The dish is widely sold at Indonesian fast-casual restaurants and food delivery platforms across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, a 400 g serving provides approximately 650 kcal, 45 g protein, 38 g fat, and 35 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is moderate-to-high at 237.5 mg per 100 g, and dietary fibre is low at 0.75 g per 100 g. The deep-frying method contributes saturated fat (2.5 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.13 g/100 g), which are clinically relevant for cardiovascular and inflammatory conditions. The dish is halal-certified in most commercial preparations. It is suitable for general consumption but should be moderated by individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis.

ingredient_synonyms.side salad: mixed fresh vegetables, typically cucumber, tomato, and lettuce

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Ayam Geprek exists in the Singapore HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy fried chicken leg data combined with a proportional salad component estimate. The input values from the GrabFood source are consistent with this composite and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Indonesian smashed fried chicken dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. The input ethnic_tags ('Stuffed Pancake', 'Pancake', 'Coffee') appear to be erroneous metadata from the source system and have been discarded. country_of_origin in the input was listed as 'Singapore' but the dish originates from Indonesia; this has been corrected to 'Indonesia'. The dish is sold in Singapore via GrabFood but is Indonesian in origin. All core macros (protein_g 11.25, fat_g 9.5, carbohydrate_g 8.75) are non-zero and consistent with a deep-fried battered chicken dish with side salad. GI of 55 is classified as 'low' per the University of Sydney GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70); previous iteration incorrectly assigned 'medium' — corrected in this iteration.
